title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.1 Working identity
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab is a small independent educational and scientific research institute focused on the economic consequences of large language models and the emerging agent economy.
Public-facing brand:

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text: gecon.ai

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.2 Mission
The mission of the lab is to advance public understanding of how large language models and AI agents affect economic behavior, institutions, and decision-making. The lab conducts independent educational and scientific research on the emerging agent economy, including how AI agents behave, how they interact with one another, and how they interact with humans as they become increasingly embedded in modern economic life.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.3 Vision
The long-run vision is to help build a rigorous field of research around the economics of generative AI and AI agents. The lab aims to become a trusted source of public-interest research, methodology, and evidence in an area that is expanding rapidly but still lacks shared standards, reproducible methods, and durable conceptual frameworks.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.9 Year 1 direction
The lab’s first year should be framed around a focused and credible initial agenda rather than a broad platform.

2.9.1 Priority 1. Publish current research
The lab aims to revise, submit, and publicly disseminate existing work, including:

Model Personality Trumps Persona Prompting: Evidence from Controlled Prompting Experiments
AI or Not? Human Inability to Reliably Detect AI-Generated Content

These projects help establish the lab’s credibility and illustrate its interest in both model behavior and human perception.


2.9.2 Priority 2. Complete the AI-or-Not pilot and experimental preparation
The lab aims to complete the AI-or-Not pilot and secure IRB approval for the related experiment. This creates a concrete path toward human-subjects research on perception, trust, and human interaction with AI-generated outputs.


2.9.3 Priority 3. Define the research agenda on LLMs as economic agents
The lab aims to develop a structured research agenda on LLMs as economic agents, including methodology, benchmarks, and questions around preferences, bias, and agent-to-agent behavior.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.10 Representative topics and paper directions

2.10.1 Survey / human versus LLM comparison

Comparing Survey Design Bias Effects Across Humans and Language Models
Who Do Language Models Represent? Evidence from Human-LLM Survey Response Comparisons
Do Language Models Exhibit Gender, Race, and Other Social Biases by Default?
Log Probabilities as a Tool for Inferring Preferences in Language Models



2.10.2 Methodology / reproducibility

Reproducibility in LLM-Based Studies: The Effects of Batching, Sampling, and Inference Configuration
Towards Reproducibility in LLM Research: A Multi-Paper Replication Study and Experimental Guidelines



2.10.3 Preferences and decision theory

Extracting Revealed vs Stated Preferences in Language Models
Preference Inference in LLMs Using Choice and Log Probability Signals



2.10.4 Agent-agent systems

Price Formation in Multi-Agent Language Model Markets
Evolution of Strategy and Knowledge in Repeated Negotiation Games with Language Models



2.10.5 Human-agent interaction

“Talk to a Human”: Acceptance of AI Agents in Call Centers Across System Architectures
Trust in AI vs Human Advisors: Evidence from Controlled Decision Tasks



2.10.6 Misinformation and development

Susceptibility to AI-Generated Misinformation in Low-Information Environments: Evidence from Text, Image, and Video Stimuli
Detection Gaps and Belief Formation: Effects of AI-Generated Political Content on User Perceptions



2.10.7 Culture / language shift

AI Speak: Convergence of Human Writing Toward Language Model Style in SAT, GRE, and GMAT Essays

title: 2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.14 Budget framing
The lean budget structure can be described in three tiers.

2.14.1 Minimum: proof of concept lab

$20k-$40k per year
focused on 1 to 2 strong studies
heavy use of credits, preprints, and founder-led execution



2.14.2 Good: sustainable research engine

$40k-$75k per year
supports 2 to 4 papers per year
enables multiple studies and regular dissemination



2.14.3 Excellent: high-output lean lab

$75k-$120k per year
supports larger experiments, more visible events, and stronger field presence

The major funding needs are:

human experiments;
compute;
publishing;
travel;
workshops and events;
light research support or contractors where necessary.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.15 Short public-facing version
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab studies how large language models and AI agents affect economic behavior, decision-making, and institutions. We conduct independent educational and scientific research on agent preferences, agent-to-agent systems, and human-agent interaction, while developing rigorous methods, datasets, and benchmarks for a rapidly emerging field.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.16 Short donor-facing version
We are building an independent nonprofit research lab focused on the economics of generative AI and the emerging agent economy. Our goal is to produce rigorous, public-interest research on how AI agents behave, how they interact with one another and with humans, and how these interactions will shape modern economies. We also aim to strengthen research quality in a fast-growing field by developing methods, benchmarks, and student-engaged research programs.

title: 2  Direction and Mission
text:
2.17 Open strategic questions
These questions still need sharper answers over time:

How broad should the lab remain versus specializing early in LLMs as economic agents?
How much of the portfolio should be human-subjects work versus agent simulation work?
How should industry collaboration be structured without creating private-benefit concerns?
What is the clearest public-charity funding story in the first 2 years?
Which outputs should define the lab most strongly: papers, benchmarks, public education, or field-building events?

title: 3  California Articles of Incorporation for a 501(c)(3)
text:
3.1 Working legal name
This is a drafting aid for the California Secretary of State ARTS-PB-501(c)(3) filing.
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
crumbs: 3  California Articles of Incorporation for a 501(c)(3)

title: 3  California Articles of Incorporation for a 501(c)(3)
text:
3.2 Draft articles text

3.2.1 Article 1. Name
The name of this corporation is:
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab


3.2.2 Article 2. Type
This corporation is a nonprofit public benefit corporation and is not organized for the private gain of any person. It is organized under the California Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Law for public, charitable, educational, and scientific purposes.


3.2.3 Article 3. Specific purpose
This corporation is organized exclusively for charitable, educational, and scientific purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or the corresponding section of any future federal tax code.
The specific purposes of this corporation include conducting and disseminating independent research on the economics of generative artificial intelligence, large language models, and AI agents; studying the emerging agent economy, including agent preferences, decision-making, biases, agent-to-agent interaction, and human-agent interaction; publishing public-interest research, datasets, benchmarks, educational materials, and policy analysis; developing and sharing research methodologies relevant to this novel and rapidly developing field; providing students with opportunities to participate in the research process and to study artificial intelligence rigorously; supporting conferences, seminars, workshops, and collaborations that advance public understanding of artificial intelligence and economics; and making distributions to organizations that qualify as exempt organizations under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.


3.2.4 Article 4. Restrictions
No part of the net earnings of this corporation shall inure to the benefit of, or be distributable to, its directors, officers, or other private persons, except that the corporation shall be authorized and empowered to pay reasonable compensation for services rendered and to make payments and distributions in furtherance of its exempt purposes.
No substantial part of the activities of this corporation shall consist of carrying on propaganda, or otherwise attempting to influence legislation, except as permitted under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, and this corporation shall not participate or intervene in any political campaign on behalf of or in opposition to any candidate for public office.
Notwithstanding any other provision of these Articles, this corporation shall not carry on any activities not permitted to be carried on by a corporation exempt from federal income tax under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.


3.2.5 Article 5. Dissolution
Upon the dissolution of this corporation, its assets remaining after payment, or provision for payment, of all debts and liabilities of this corporation shall be distributed for one or more exempt purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or shall be distributed to the federal government, or to a state or local government, for a public purpose.


3.2.6 Article 6. Initial street address
The initial street address of this corporation is:
320B 2nd Ave, San Francisco, California 94118


3.2.7 Article 7. Initial mailing address
The initial mailing address of this corporation, if different from the street address, is:
Same as above


3.2.8 Article 8. Initial agent for service of process
The name and California street address of the corporation’s initial agent for service of process are:
Name: Nikita Tkachenko Address: 320B 2nd Ave, San Francisco, California 94118
crumbs: 3  California Articles of Incorporation for a 501(c)(3)

text:

Unanimous Written Consent of the Board of Directors of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

crumbs: 5  Initial Board Action by Unanimous Written Consent

title: 5  Initial Board Action by Unanimous Written Consent
text:
5.1.1 1. Ratification of formation
RESOLVED, that the filing of the Articles of Incorporation is ratified and approved.


5.1.2 2. Bylaws
RESOLVED, that the Bylaws presented to the Board are adopted as the Bylaws of the corporation, to be placed in the corporate records book.


5.1.3 3. Principal office
RESOLVED, that the principal office of the corporation shall be located at 320B 2nd Ave, San Francisco, California 94118, or such other location as the Board may later approve.


5.1.4 4. Fiscal year
RESOLVED, that the fiscal year of the corporation shall end on [MONTH DAY].
Recommended default if no reason otherwise:
December 31


5.1.5 5. Election of officers
RESOLVED, that the following persons are elected to the offices set forth opposite their names, to serve at the pleasure of the Board:

President: Nikita Tkachenko
Secretary: Jake Cosgrove
Treasurer: Alessandsa Cassar



5.1.6 6. Banking authority
RESOLVED, that the officers are authorized to open one or more bank accounts in the name of the corporation and execute such documents as are required by the financial institution, subject to any further limits the Board later adopts.


5.1.7 7. EIN and tax filings
RESOLVED, that the officers are authorized to obtain an EIN and prepare, sign, and submit all federal, state, and local registrations, elections, and exemption filings, including IRS Form 1023 or 1023-EZ, California Form SI-100, Attorney General charitable registration materials, and FTB exemption filings.


5.1.8 8. Conflict of interest policy
RESOLVED, that the Conflict of Interest Policy presented to the Board is adopted, and each director and officer shall complete annual disclosures.


5.1.9 9. Compensation review policy
RESOLVED, that the Compensation Review Policy presented to the Board is adopted and shall govern compensation arrangements with directors, officers, key employees, and other disqualified persons.


5.1.10 10. Records and minute book
RESOLVED, that the Secretary is directed to maintain complete and accurate corporate records, including articles, bylaws, minutes, consents, policies, exemption filings, and annual reports.


5.1.11 11. General authority
RESOLVED, that the officers of the corporation are authorized to take any additional actions and execute any additional documents reasonably necessary to carry out the intent of these resolutions.
This consent may be executed in counterparts and electronically.
Date: [DATE]
Director signatures:
__________________________________ Nikita Tkachenko
__________________________________ Jake Cosgrove
__________________________________ Alessandsa Cassar

title: 6  Director and Officer Acknowledgment and Consent
text:
The undersigned consents to serve in the role indicated below for Economics of Generative AI Research Lab, a California nonprofit public benefit corporation.
The undersigned has reviewed, or will review before signing final formation documents, the Articles of Incorporation, Bylaws, Conflict of Interest Policy, and initial board resolutions.
The undersigned understands that directors are responsible for the governance of the corporation, including oversight of mission, compliance, conflicts of interest, financial stewardship, and major organizational decisions.
Any undersigned serving as an officer understands the responsibilities of that office, including the duties customarily associated with that office and any additional duties assigned by the Board.
The undersigned agrees to act in good faith, in a manner believed to be in the best interests of the corporation, and in furtherance of its educational and scientific purposes.
The undersigned agrees to disclose actual or potential conflicts of interest and to comply with the corporation’s governing documents and board-approved policies.
The undersigned understands that the corporation is intended to operate as a nonprofit organization organized exclusively for educational and scientific purposes and not as a consulting business, product business, startup, or other company-like venture.

text:

The principal office of the corporation shall be located at such place in California as the Board of Directors may determine. The corporation may maintain such other offices as the Board may establish.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.3 Article 3. Purpose and limitations
This corporation is organized and shall be operated exclusively for charitable, educational, and scientific purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
No part of the net earnings of this corporation shall inure to the benefit of, or be distributable to, any private person, except that the corporation may pay reasonable compensation for services rendered and make payments in furtherance of its exempt purposes.
No substantial part of the activities of this corporation shall consist of carrying on propaganda or otherwise attempting to influence legislation, except as permitted under Section 501(c)(3), and the corporation shall not participate or intervene in any political campaign on behalf of or in opposition to any candidate for public office.
The corporation shall not engage in activities not permitted to be carried on by a corporation exempt from federal income tax under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:

This corporation shall have no members within the meaning of the California Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Law.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.5 Article 5. Board of Directors
The corporation shall be governed by a Board of Directors.
The authorized number of directors shall be not less than 3 and not more than 7, with the exact number to be fixed from time to time by Board resolution.
Directors shall serve terms of 2 years and may be re-elected.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.6 Article 6. Powers of the Board
Subject to law and the Articles of Incorporation, all corporate powers shall be exercised by or under the authority of the Board, and the business and affairs of the corporation shall be managed under the direction of the Board.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.7 Article 7. Meetings
Regular meetings of the Board may be held at such time and place as the Board determines.
Special meetings may be called by the President, the Secretary, or any 2 directors.
Notice of special meetings shall be given at least 4 days before the meeting by mail or 48 hours before the meeting by electronic transmission, personal delivery, or telephone.
Meetings may be held by conference telephone, video, or similar communications equipment so long as all directors can hear one another.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.8 Article 8. Quorum and voting
A majority of the authorized number of directors then in office shall constitute a quorum.
If a quorum is present, the act of a majority of directors present shall be the act of the Board, unless a greater vote is required by law, the Articles, or these Bylaws.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.9 Article 9. Action without meeting
Any action required or permitted to be taken by the Board may be taken without a meeting if all directors consent in writing or by electronic transmission.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.10 Article 10. Vacancies
Any vacancy on the Board may be filled by approval of the Board or, if the number of directors then in office is less than a quorum, by unanimous written consent of the directors then in office.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.11 Article 11. Officers
The officers of the corporation shall be a President, a Secretary, and a Treasurer, and may include such other officers as the Board appoints.
The same person may hold any number of offices except that neither the Secretary nor the Treasurer may serve concurrently as President.

9.11.1 President
The President shall be the chief executive officer unless the Board provides otherwise and shall supervise the general affairs of the corporation.


9.11.2 Secretary
The Secretary shall keep or cause to be kept the minutes of proceedings of the Board, maintain corporate records, and give required notices.


9.11.3 Treasurer
The Treasurer shall oversee the corporation’s financial affairs, books, reports, and funds.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.12 Article 12. Committees
The Board may create committees to the extent permitted by California law. No committee may exercise the authority of the Board except as expressly allowed by law and Board resolution.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.13 Article 13. Conflict of interest
The corporation shall maintain a conflict of interest policy applicable to directors, officers, committee members with board-delegated powers, and key employees. Interested-director transactions shall be reviewed and approved only in accordance with California law and applicable federal tax-exemption requirements.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.14 Article 14. Compensation
Directors shall not receive compensation for service as directors unless specifically approved by the Board in compliance with the corporation’s compensation review policy and applicable law. Directors may receive reasonable reimbursement of expenses.
Any compensation paid by the corporation to officers, employees, contractors, or other insiders shall be reasonable and in furtherance of the corporation’s exempt purposes.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
text:
9.15 Article 15. Indemnification
To the fullest extent permitted by California law, the corporation shall indemnify its directors, officers, employees, and agents for acts performed in good faith in the course and scope of their service to the corporation.
crumbs: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

title: 9  Bylaws of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab

The corporation shall maintain correct books and records of account, minutes of proceedings of the Board and committees, and such records as required by law.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.1 1. Purpose
The purpose of this policy is to protect the interests of Economics of Generative AI Research Lab when it is considering a transaction or arrangement that may benefit the private interest of a director, officer, key employee, or other person in a position to exercise substantial influence over the affairs of the corporation.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.2 2. Covered persons
This policy applies to directors, officers, committee members with board-delegated powers, key employees, and any other person designated by the Board.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.4 4. Duty to disclose
Covered persons shall disclose actual, potential, and apparent conflicts to the Board or relevant committee before discussion or decision on the matter.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.5 5. Determination procedure
After disclosure of the material facts, the interested person shall leave the meeting while the remaining disinterested directors discuss and determine whether a conflict exists and, if so, whether the proposed transaction is fair, reasonable, and in the corporation’s best interests.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.7 7. Compensation matters
A voting member of the Board who receives compensation from the corporation may provide factual information regarding compensation arrangements but shall not vote on that person’s own compensation.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.9 9. Violations
If the Board has reasonable cause to believe a covered person failed to disclose an actual or possible conflict, the Board shall inform the person of the basis for that belief and allow an opportunity to explain. If, after investigation, the Board determines a violation occurred, it may take appropriate corrective action.

title: 10  Conflict of Interest Policy
10  Conflict of Interest Policy
text:
10.11 Annual disclosure form
Name: [NAME]
Role: [ROLE]
Disclosed entities / relationships: [LIST OR NONE]
Signature: ____________________________
Date: [DATE]

title: 11  Compensation Review Policy
text:
11.1 Purpose
The corporation will establish compensation only when it is reasonable, aligned with exempt purposes, and approved through a documented process that reduces private inurement and excess benefit risk.

title: 11  Compensation Review Policy
11  Compensation Review Policy
11  Compensation Review Policy
text:
11.4 Interested persons
Any person with a conflict relating to the compensation decision shall disclose the conflict and recuse from voting.

title: 11  Compensation Review Policy
11  Compensation Review Policy
11  Compensation Review Policy
text:
11.7 Guiding rule
The corporation should default to conservative, market-grounded compensation and avoid arrangements tied primarily to revenue generation, private fundraising percentages, or founder discretion.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.1 Organization overview
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab is a California nonprofit public benefit corporation organized exclusively for educational and scientific purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
The corporation’s mission is to advance public understanding of how large language models and AI agents affect economic behavior, decision-making, and institutions. The organization conducts independent educational and scientific research on the emerging agent economy, including how AI agents behave, how they interact with one another, and how they interact with humans as they become increasingly embedded in modern economic life.
The organization is operated exclusively for educational and scientific purposes and does not intend to conduct consulting, product development, licensing, paid private-client research, or other commercial business activity.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
section: 12.2 Activity 1. Research on agents as objects of study

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
section: 12.3 Activity 2. Research on agent-to-agent interaction and the emerging agent economy

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
section: 12.4 Activity 3. Research on human-agent interaction and public perception

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
section: 12.5 Activity 4. Methodology, benchmarks, and research quality

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.6 Public dissemination and educational engagement
The organization intends to publish papers, working papers, public research summaries, and methodological materials, and may also present work through workshops and lectures. Its educational approach includes involving students directly in the research process and making research outputs available to appropriate public and academic audiences.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.7 Revenue sources
Expected revenue sources may include:

founder donations;
research grants;
foundation grants.

The organization does not intend to conduct commercial activity, including consulting services, product development, licensing businesses, or other company-like operations. Its activities are limited to research, education, publication, and related exempt functions.
The organization may collaborate with academic institutions, nonprofit organizations, and, in limited ways, for-profit companies where such collaboration advances public-interest research. Any interaction with for-profit companies will be structured so that private parties do not control research conclusions, research design, or publication decisions.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.8 Compensation and private benefit
The organization does not currently plan to compensate directors or officers during the first 12 months. If compensation is ever introduced, it will be set under a documented review process using disinterested approval and reasonableness standards.
No part of the organization’s net earnings will inure to insiders. The organization will not be operated for the benefit of founders, directors, donors, or any other private party.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.9 Political activity and lobbying
The organization will not participate or intervene in political campaigns. It will not devote a substantial part of its activities to lobbying.

title: 12  Form 1023 activity narrative
text:
12.10 Geographic scope
Activities will primarily be conducted in the United States through online collaboration, academic collaboration, and work connected to the San Francisco principal office.

14.1 Supporting factual notes
This is an internal working summary of the first-year activity mix reflected in the narrative draft.

title: 14  Internal activity table for lawyer review
text:
Public outputs expected in year 1: 1-2 papers, working papers, and methodological materials as feasible
Events expected in year 1: workshops and lectures
Student participation: may include research assistant, volunteer, fellow, workshop participant, or coauthor roles depending on collaboration structure
No planned director or officer compensation in year 1
No planned grants or fellowships in year 1
No planned software/tool development in year 1
No planned consulting, paid private-client research, product sales, licensing business, or startup activity

title: 15  Budget
text:
15.1 1023-EZ
This section presents four budget scenarios for planning and filing discussion:
The 1023-EZ scenario is the only one designed to remain under the projected gross-receipts ceiling associated with 1023-EZ. The other three scenarios are useful for strategic planning and for evaluating whether the full Form 1023 is the more realistic filing path.
This scenario is designed to remain consistent with a 1023-EZ posture by keeping projected annual gross receipts under $50,000 in each of the next 3 years.

title: 15  Budget
text:
15.1.1 Year 1

15.1.1.1 Revenue

Founder donations: $15,000
Foundation grants: $10,000
Other contributions: $5,000

Total revenue: $30,000


15.1.1.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $15,000
Publishing / open access: $3,000
Website / software / tools: $2,000
Professional fees: $2,000
Travel: $4,000
Events / workshops: $2,000
Insurance / admin / other: $2,000

Total expenses: $30,000
Net: $0



15.1.2 Year 2

15.1.2.1 Revenue

Individual contributions: $15,000
Foundation grants: $15,000
Research support / grants: $10,000
Other contributions: $5,000

Total revenue: $45,000


15.1.2.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $22,000
Publishing / open access: $5,000
Website / software / tools: $3,000
Professional fees: $2,000
Travel: $6,000
Events / workshops: $4,000
Insurance / admin / other: $3,000

Total expenses: $45,000
Net: $0



15.1.3 Year 3

15.1.3.1 Revenue

Individual contributions: $15,000
Foundation grants: $15,000
Research support / grants: $10,000
Other contributions: $8,000

Total revenue: $48,000


15.1.3.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $24,000
Publishing / open access: $6,000
Website / software / tools: $3,000
Professional fees: $2,000
Travel: $7,000
Events / workshops: $4,000
Insurance / admin / other: $2,000

Total expenses: $48,000
Net: $0



15.1.4 Notes

This scenario is the only one in this packet designed to stay within 1023-EZ gross-receipts assumptions.
It supports a lean research program while preserving 1023-EZ eligibility assumptions on projected receipts.

title: 15  Budget
text:
15.2 Good
This scenario reflects a sustainable research engine with regular output and moderate field visibility.

15.2.1 Year 1

15.2.1.1 Revenue

Founder donations: $20,000
Foundation grants: $20,000
Research support / grants: $10,000
Other contributions: $5,000

Total revenue: $55,000


15.2.1.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $25,000
Publishing / open access: $7,000
Website / software / tools: $3,000
Professional fees: $2,000
Travel: $8,000
Events / workshops: $4,000
Insurance / admin / other: $6,000

Total expenses: $55,000
Net: $0



15.2.2 Notes

This scenario supports 2 to 4 papers per year.
Because projected receipts exceed $50,000, it is more consistent with full Form 1023 than 1023-EZ.

title: 15  Budget
text:
15.3 Great
This scenario reflects a stronger field-building research program with larger experimental capacity and wider dissemination.

15.3.1 Year 1

15.3.1.1 Revenue

Individual contributions: $25,000
Foundation grants: $30,000
Research support / grants: $20,000
Other contributions: $10,000

Total revenue: $85,000


15.3.1.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $38,000
Publishing / open access: $10,000
Website / software / tools: $4,000
Professional fees: $2,000
Travel: $12,000
Events / workshops: $8,000
Contractors / research support: $6,000
Insurance / admin / other: $5,000

Total expenses: $85,000
Net: $0



15.3.2 Notes

This scenario fits a visible and productive lean lab with expanded study capacity.
It clearly fits full Form 1023, not 1023-EZ.

title: 15  Budget
text:
15.4 Excellent
This scenario reflects a high-output lean lab with strong field visibility, frequent dissemination, and larger human-subjects capacity.

15.4.1 Year 1

15.4.1.1 Revenue

Individual contributions: $30,000
Foundation grants: $40,000
Research support / grants: $30,000
Other contributions: $10,000

Total revenue: $110,000


15.4.1.2 Expenses

Human experiments: $50,000
Publishing / open access: $15,000
Website / software / tools: $5,000
Professional fees: $3,000
Travel: $15,000
Events / workshops: $10,000
Contractors / research support: $7,000
Insurance / admin / other: $5,000

Total expenses: $110,000
Net: $0



15.4.2 Notes

This scenario supports a high-output research lab with stronger field presence and more ambitious dissemination.
It is appropriate only for full Form 1023, not 1023-EZ.

title: 16  Donation acknowledgment template
text:
Economics of Generative AI Research Lab
Date: [DATE]
Dear [DONOR NAME],
Thank you for your contribution to Economics of Generative AI Research Lab in the amount of $[AMOUNT] received on [DATE].
No goods or services were provided in exchange for this contribution, other than intangible religious benefits, if any.
[OR, IF SOMETHING WAS PROVIDED: The estimated fair market value of goods or services provided in exchange for this contribution was $[AMOUNT].]
Your support helps advance educational and scientific research on the economics of generative artificial intelligence and related technologies.
Sincerely,
[NAME]
[TITLE]
